title: Markets Are Pricing Systems, Not Headlines
summary: For long-term readers, the useful question is not whether a headline is dramatic, but whether it changes liquidity, earnings durability, policy expectations, or risk appetite.
imageAlt: Editorial illustration of market signals and capital flows
imageCaption: Market analysis improves when headlines are mapped to mechanisms.

body.text: Financial news is loud because markets are always processing uncertainty. The better discipline is to treat each headline as an input into a system rather than as a complete explanation.

items: Does the news change expected cash flows or only the mood around them?
Does it affect liquidity, credit conditions, or the cost of capital?
Does it alter policy expectations in a way that changes discount rates?
Does it reveal durable positioning, or just a short burst of attention?

body.text: This framework helps separate durable information from market theater. A dramatic story can be financially small; a quiet rate, margin, inventory, or guidance change can be financially large.
The strongest finance writing connects narrative to mechanism. It explains why something matters, what would confirm or disconfirm it, and which second-order effects deserve attention.
This site treats finance coverage as informational analysis, not investment advice. The goal is to make readers more precise about risk, incentives, and time horizons.
